title: Interest rates: Contrarians tip more RBA raising
full_text:
They may be an increasingly small number, but a defiant minority of economists say they remain convinced that the Reserve Bank of Australia will increase interest rates despite market conviction they have peaked.
Faraz Syed, a senior economist at Citi, is leading the contrarian charge and says he believes that the market is misjudging the situation.
"The market pricing suggests that the RBA is done raising rates, but we think it's underpricing the risk of elevated underlying inflation," Syed said.
His warning comes after the central bank has lifted the cash rate three consecutive times this year to tame resurging inflation. On Tuesday, it left rates at 4.35 per cent, as expected, with policymakers citing weakening economic growth, a stalled housing market and rising unemployment.
Following the decision, bond traders pared back bets of further increases. Markets say there is less than a 50 per cent chance the central bank will lift rates this year, down from total certainty of a hike earlier in the week.
Traders have even begun to entertain the prospect of a rate cut, flagging a 40 per cent chance of lower borrowing costs late next year.
This optimism has seen heavyweights such as National Australia Bank and Commonwealth Bank abandon calls for tightening over the past month following a run of softer domestic data on inflation, consumer demand and employment. Yet, a handful of forecasters refuse to shift.
Economists at Citi, Westpac, AMP, UBS, Barrenjoey, Goldman Sachs, Judo, and Rabobank are among the small group predicting the central bank's next move is still up, not down.
The RBA led developed economies in raising interest rates as it struggled to contain runaway inflation, which hit 4.2 per cent in the 12 months to March, well above the 2.5 per cent at the midpoint of its target range.
According to Syed, upcoming quarterly inflation data is likely to show a re-acceleration that will be impossible for policymakers to ignore.
"It will take a very brave central bank to look through six months of very high inflation and then not to hike again," he said, adding that slow-moving but elevated fuel prices and supply chain disruptions from the war in the Middle East would push up food costs.
This concern over the RBA's credibility is a recurring theme. The central bank has repeatedly pushed out its timeline for returning inflation to target, and there is a growing sense that its patience is running thin.
Andrew Boak, Goldman Sachs' local chief economist, is tipping a fourth interest rate rise in August after the release of the June quarter data.
"It looks like we're going to get another strong print. And not responding with a tighter policy would be complicated and risky for a central bank that's very sensitive to criticism that it's missing its inflation target," he said.
Geopolitics is also playing a role in keeping the hawks on alert. Rabobank senior strategist Ben Picton warned that a rate increase remained on the table due to his scepticism over the apparent end of the Iran war.
He cautioned that negotiations between Washington and Tehran could collapse, potentially closing the crucial Strait of Hormuz. This shipping route, critical for fuel and materials supply, is due to reopen immediately.
"We're worried that we might see another leg higher in energy prices and goods further down the supply chain," he said, even as Brent crude prices fell below $US80 a barrel for the first time since the conflict started. Oil prices spiked above $US120 in April.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Cecile Lefort
heading: Banking and Economy
